ocr.text: Table 3. Teaching topic core modules, as established by the NGS Trainers Consortium during the “Best Practice in HTS data analysis” workshop.
Topic Level Module Name Module objectives Essential
/Optional
Prerequisite Basic Linux Develop a familiarity with the Linux command line environment, such as Essential
navigating folders, learn what commands and parameters are and how to use
them, open ﬁles on a terminal.
R-programming Describe the R programming environment. Perform basic programming in R Essential
Statistics Review the basic statistical concepts Essential
HTS Basic Technologies Deﬁne the principles of high throughput sequencing technologies Essential
Introduction
Data Formats Describe the different data formats commonly used for HTS data Essential
ChIP-Seq Advanced Preprocessing Describe necessary preprocessing steps, Perform a Quality Assessment and Essential
interpret the results
Alignment Perform alignment, Discuss alignment considerations Essential
ChIP-Seq-QC Explain ChIP-Seq speciﬁc QC steps, Perform ChIP-Seq QC on a data set Essential
Peak-calling List appropriate peak-calling software, Describe the theoretical basis of peak- Essential
calling, Apply different peak callers
Visualization Visualize raw and processed data, Assess data quality Essential
Annotation Interpret results in the genomic context Optional
Differential binding Perform DB analysis, Interpret the output, Recognize the need for normalization Optional
(DB)
Working with biological Compare and combine different biological replicates using IDR analysis Optional
replicates
Non-peak based Inspect signal around regions of interest, Generate carpet plots Optional
analysis
RNA-Seq Advanced Preprocessing Apply QC software and interpret the output, Decide/perform necessary Essential
preprocessing steps
Alignment Distinguish between genome and transcriptome alignment, Select the Essential
appropriate tool, Recognize the challenges and pitfalls, Produce alignment,
Interpret the alignment ﬁle
Alignment QC Apply QC software, Interpret the output, Decide/perform necessary ﬁltering steps Essential
Feature summarization Produce a table of counts, Identify a proper strategy for the biological question, Essential
Interpret output
Exploratory analysis Visualize alignments, Applying QC software (e.g. clustering, Principal Essential
Component Analysis (PCA), etc.), Identify confounding effects and take
necessary action
De-novo transcriptome Perform the analysis, Recognize the challenges and pitfalls, Interpret the Optional
assembly assemblers output
Differential expression Perform DE analysis, Interpret the output, Recognize the need for normalization Optional
(DE) and dispersion estimation
Variant Advanced Preprocessing Apply QC software and interpret the output, Decide/perform necessary Essential
analysis preprocessing steps
Alignment Differentiate genome and transcriptome alignment, Select the appropriate tool, Essential
Recognize the challenges and pitfalls, Produce alignment, Interpret the aligners
output
Alignment QC Apply QC software and interpret the output, Decide/perform necessary ﬁltering Essential
steps
Variant Calling Apply variant calling software, Understand the format and the different type of Essential
variants
Variant Analysis Visualize variant calls, Interpret the output, Select/ﬁlter variants Essential
Annotating Variants Annotate variants, Lookup potential effects on coding regions, Evaluate putative Optional
clinical relevance
The level column details what the target audience of individual topics should be. The optional modules are the ones that can vary based on the course
program and duration. The essential modules are the ones that are considered mandatory for any course addressing the corresponding topic; i.e., they
are the core set of modules, common to all downstream analyses, that a trainee must learn about.
